issues.description: The test `duplicate_template_names_emits_diagnostic` filters for diagnostics by message content but never asserts `d.severity == reify_types::Severity::Error`. The implementation correctly uses `Diagnostic::error(...)`, but if it were accidentally changed to `Diagnostic::warning(...)` the test would still pass. The companion test `duplicate_sub_refs_deduped` (line 339-354) in the same module explicitly checks severity — this test should follow the same pattern. Duplicate-name detection is an error-level condition (it corrupts the adjacency graph), so the severity is semantically important.
issues.suggested_fix: Change the filter to also assert severity: `diagnostics.iter().filter(|d| d.severity == reify_types::Severity::Error && d.message.to_lowercase().contains("duplicate"))`. Also assert the count is exactly 1 (two templates → one collision).

issues.description: There is no test covering the combination of a duplicate template name alongside a legitimate cycle. For example: templates [A, B, A] where A→B→A forms a cycle. The expected behavior is: one `Error` diagnostic for the duplicate, one `Warning` diagnostic for the cycle, and both A (index 0) and B marked `is_recursive`. Without this test, it is unverified that the duplicate-detection path does not interfere with Tarjan's SCC analysis on the remaining valid templates.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test `duplicate_name_does_not_suppress_cycle_detection`: create templates [A(subs:[B]), B(subs:[A]), A(no subs)], run `detect_recursive_structures`, assert one error diagnostic containing 'duplicate', one warning diagnostic containing 'recursive structure cycle', and `templates[0].is_recursive && templates[1].is_recursive`.

issues.description: There is no test for three or more templates sharing the same name (e.g., [A, A, A]). The code's loop should emit N-1 error diagnostics for N templates with the same name, but this is unverified. A subtle off-by-one or early-return mistake would not be caught by the two-template test.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test with three templates sharing name 'A' and assert that exactly 2 error diagnostics containing 'duplicate' are emitted.

issues.description: There is no end-to-end integration test that compiles source containing duplicate structure names and verifies the `detect_recursive_structures` duplicate-name diagnostic flows through the full `compile_all()` pipeline. All 15 existing integration tests in `recursive_detection_tests.rs` exercise normal-path inputs. If a downstream pipeline stage swallows or transforms the error diagnostic, this would go undetected.
issues.suggested_fix: Add an integration test to `recursive_detection_tests.rs` that compiles a module defining two structures with the same name and asserts an `Error`-severity diagnostic is present in the returned diagnostics.

test_analyst.summary: The diff adds duplicate-template-name detection to `detect_recursive_structures` and ships with a new inline unit test. The test correctly exercises the happy path (two templates with the same name produce a diagnostic), but it has four coverage gaps. Most critically, it never asserts the diagnostic's severity is `Error` — the sibling test `duplicate_sub_refs_deduped` in the same file explicitly checks severity, but the new test does not, meaning an accidental severity downgrade to `Warning` would go undetected. There is also no test for the combination of duplicate names alongside legitimate cycles (unverified that duplicate detection doesn't corrupt the SCC pass for valid templates), no test for N > 2 duplicates, and no integration test through the full compilation pipeline. All four issues are suggestions; none are blocking.

issues.description: The new duplicate-name guard is unreachable in the actual call path. `lib.rs:3897-3902` already filters out duplicates before pushing to `templates`: `is_first_def` is false for any duplicate structure, so it is never compiled or appended. By the time `detect_recursive_structures` is called at `lib.rs:4049`, the slice is guaranteed to contain no duplicate names. The new error diagnostic path therefore duplicates upstream validation logic that is already enforced, and the error can never be emitted during normal compilation. The associated unit test (`duplicate_template_names_emits_diagnostic`) exercises the guard in isolation, but that isolation scenario cannot arise from the public compiler entry points.
issues.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a comment like `// defensive: upstream pass 2 (lib.rs:3897-3902) already guarantees uniqueness, but guard here so scc is correct if called from other contexts` to make the intent explicit, or (b) remove the guard and replace it with a `debug_assert!` that fires in tests if a duplicate somehow slips through, keeping the hot path simple and the responsibility clearly owned by the elaboration phase.

reuse_auditor.summary: The diff introduces a sound improvement — detecting duplicate template names and emitting a diagnostic rather than silently overwriting the HashMap entry (which would corrupt the adjacency graph). The implementation pattern (explicit loop with `.get()` + `.insert()`) is consistent with the dominant duplicate-detection style used for `seen_entity_names` in `lib.rs`. However, the guard is dead code in the current call path: `lib.rs:3897-3902` already filters out duplicate structures via an `is_first_def` check before they are compiled and pushed to `templates`, so `detect_recursive_structures` is always called with a duplicate-free slice. The main concern is that this creates two places responsible for the same invariant (unique template names) without the relationship being documented, which could confuse future maintainers. A clarifying comment or replacing the guard with a `debug_assert!` would address this without any functional risk.

issues.description: detect_recursive_structures now performs two distinct responsibilities: template uniqueness validation and cycle detection. The codebase already has a dedicated duplicate-name detection pass earlier in the pipeline (lib.rs ~line 3571, via seen_entity_names with dual-location SourceSpan labels). Embedding a second duplicate check inside the SCC function conflates validation with graph analysis. If duplicates survive the upstream pass to reach SCC, that represents a compiler invariant violation — this defensive check is reasonable but belongs in a dedicated validate_template_uniqueness helper (or annotated as an internal assertion), not woven into the graph-building preamble of the SCC function.
issues.suggested_fix: Extract the duplicate detection into a small private helper, e.g. build_name_index(templates, diagnostics) -> HashMap<&str, usize>, making the SCC function's intent clear. Alternatively, document it with a comment like // INVARIANT: upstream pass should have caught these; this is a safety net to prevent graph corruption.

issues.description: The duplicate-name diagnostic uses raw array indices: "duplicate template name '{}': indices {} and {}". These are internal compiler positions, meaningless to users. The established diagnostic pattern throughout the compiler (e.g., the parse-level duplicate check at lib.rs ~3571) includes SourceSpan labels via .with_label() so users see both definition sites in context. TopologyTemplate has no span field directly, but SubComponentDecl and other related types carry SourceSpan. The SCC-level diagnostic is strictly less informative than the upstream one and may confuse users who see it without a source location.
issues.suggested_fix: Either (a) use a richer message that omits the internal indices (e.g., "duplicate template name '{}' — first definition will be used"), or (b) if TopologyTemplate gains a span field, attach it via .with_label() to match the dual-location pattern used elsewhere. As a minimal fix, omit the indices: format!("duplicate template name '{}'", t.name).

architect_reviewer.summary: The change is architecturally sound: replacing a silent-overwrite .collect() with an explicit duplicate-detecting loop correctly prevents adjacency graph corruption and is consistent with the codebase's "defense in depth" validation philosophy. Two design suggestions are raised. First, detect_recursive_structures is acquiring a secondary validation responsibility that already exists in the upstream parse pass (lib.rs ~3571 with seen_entity_names); the duplicate-detection logic would be cleaner in a private build_name_index helper, keeping the SCC function focused on cycle analysis. Second, the new diagnostic message exposes internal array indices ("indices {} and {}") rather than source spans — the established compiler pattern attaches SourceSpan labels via .with_label() so users see both definition sites; the SCC diagnostic should either drop the opaque indices or match the richer dual-location format used elsewhere. Neither issue causes a runtime error or API breakage, but both erode the architectural clarity and diagnostic consistency of the module.

issues.description: The duplicate-detection loop performs two hash lookups per non-duplicate entry: one for `name_to_idx.get(t.name.as_str())` and a second for `name_to_idx.insert(t.name.as_str(), i)`. The standard `Entry` API collapses this into a single hash lookup.
issues.suggested_fix: Use `match name_to_idx.entry(t.name.as_str()) { std::collections::hash_map::Entry::Occupied(e) => { diagnostics.push(/*dup error with e.get()*/); } std::collections::hash_map::Entry::Vacant(e) => { e.insert(i); } }` — one hash computation and one probe instead of two.

issues.description: The self-loop check `adjacency[v].contains(&v)` performs a linear O(degree) scan. The adjacency vectors are already `sort_unstable`+`dedup`'d earlier in the same function (lines 43-44), so a `binary_search` would give O(log degree) with no additional allocation.

For every cyclic SCC, a `HashSet<usize>` is built twice from identical data: once inside `detect_recursive_structures` (line 84, `let scc_set: HashSet<usize> = scc.iter().copied().collect()`) and again inside `reconstruct_scc_cycle` (line 205, same expression). These sets are allocated and discarded separately per cycle, doubling allocator pressure for cyclic SCCs.
issues.suggested_fix: Build `scc_set` once in `detect_recursive_structures` and pass it as a parameter to `reconstruct_scc_cycle`, changing its signature to `fn reconstruct_scc_cycle(scc: &[usize], scc_set: &HashSet<usize>, adjacency: &[Vec<usize>], templates: &[TopologyTemplate]) -> String`.

performance.summary: The diff correctly replaces the silent-overwrite `.collect()` with an explicit duplicate-detection loop, but introduces a minor inefficiency: the non-duplicate path performs two hash lookups (get + insert) where the Entry API would use one. Two additional suggestions arise from the surrounding context shown in the diff: the sorted adjacency list is scanned linearly for the self-loop check instead of using binary_search, and the scc_set HashSet is constructed twice per cyclic SCC (once in detect_recursive_structures and once inside reconstruct_scc_cycle), doubling allocation cost for the cycle-reporting path. None of these are blocking — the function is called once per compile and operates over template counts that are small in practice — but all three are straightforward single-line fixes.

issues.description: After emitting an error diagnostic for a duplicate template name, the function continues building the adjacency graph and returns `cyclic_sccs` as if the input were valid. The downstream `check_recursive_termination` pass (lib.rs:4053) runs unconditionally on this result. The interaction is safe in practice — the duplicate template (index `i`) is absent from `name_to_idx`, so no other template can form an edge pointing to it; Tarjan therefore leaves it as a trivial SCC with `is_recursive = false`; and `check_recursive_termination` skips it at its `if !template.is_recursive` guard. However, this safety argument is implicit and relies on three independent invariants holding together. A comment in `detect_recursive_structures` (or a `debug_assert!` after the loop) stating "templates with duplicate names are unreachable in the adjacency graph and will have is_recursive=false" would make the reasoning explicit and prevent future breakage if any of those invariants changes.
issues.suggested_fix: After the name-building loop, add a comment (or debug_assert) such as: `// Invariant: any template whose name was a duplicate has no entry in name_to_idx. No other template can form an adjacency edge to it, so it will be a trivial SCC with is_recursive=false and will be safely skipped by check_recursive_termination.`

issues.description: The error message `"duplicate template name '{}': indices {} and {}"` exposes raw array indices (prev_idx, i) that are internal implementation details with no meaning to a user or caller inspecting the diagnostic. `TopologyTemplate` has no `span` field so source locations aren't directly available here, but the indices provide no actionable information either — the name alone is sufficient to identify the problem. Additionally, if three or more templates share the same name, the message will report each duplicate pair separately (e.g., "indices 0 and 1" then "indices 0 and 2") which is correct but slightly redundant without source context.
issues.suggested_fix: Drop the index suffix: `format!("duplicate template name '{}'", t.name)`. If source location is later added to `TopologyTemplate`, attach it as a `DiagnosticLabel` pointing to the conflicting declaration spans instead.

robustness.summary: The change correctly replaces a silent last-wins `HashMap::collect()` (which could corrupt the adjacency graph for duplicate template names) with an explicit loop that emits an error diagnostic and deterministically keeps the first occurrence. The core logic is sound: duplicate templates are excluded from `name_to_idx`, making them unreachable in the Tarjan adjacency graph and ensuring they remain `is_recursive = false`, which causes them to be safely skipped by the downstream `check_recursive_termination` pass. No blocking issues were found. Two suggestions are raised: (1) the safety invariant connecting the duplicate-exclusion in `name_to_idx`, the isolated-vertex outcome in the adjacency graph, and the `is_recursive` guard in the termination-check pass is implicit and undocumented — a comment or debug_assert would protect against future regressions; (2) the diagnostic message includes raw array indices that are meaningless to users and should be dropped until source-span information is available on `TopologyTemplate`.
